content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660         322-5901         322-5441        322-6444

January 22, 1980


Preston W. Hill
Town Attorney
P.O. Box 949
Los Gatos, CA  95030

Dear Mr. Hill:

     This is to confirm advice given to you by telephone
by John Giansello concerning the responsibilities of
Mr. Peter Siemens under the Political Reform Act, Government
Code Sections 81000, et seq.1/  The following advice is pro-
vided pursuant to Section 83114(b).  The facts as I understand
them are as follows.

     Mr. Siemens is a member of the Los Gatos City Council,
which has before it a proposal to enact a rent control ordi-
nance.  He owns residential real property which consists of
the home in which he lives and, on the same lot, a small
dwelling which he rents out for $220 per month.  You have
asked whether Mr. Siemens can participate in the City Council's
consideration of the ordinance.

     Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act prohibits
public officials from participating in decisions in which they
have a financial interest.  Section 87103 provides that an
official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have "a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally" on various kinds of interests held by the
official, including interests in real property worth more
than $1,000.  By regulation, the Commission has determined


_____________________________________________________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise stated.

Mr. Preston W. Hill                                    Page Two
January 22, 1980


that:

     A material financial effect of a governmental
     decision on an official's interests . . . is
     distinguishable from its effect on the public
     generally unless the decision will effect the
     official's interest in substantially the same
     manner as it will affect all members of the
     public or a significant segment of the public. . . .

                    2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703

     The Commission has already issued a formal opinion inter-
preting this provision in the context of rent control ordinances
and has reached the following conclusion:

     . . . In order to be considered a significant
     segment of the public, we think a group usually
     must be large in numbers and heterogeneous in
     quality. The class of persons owning three or
     fewer units meets both these standards and
     therefore constitutes a significant segment
     of the general public . . . .

                    Opinion requested by
                    John Ferraro, 4 FPPC Opinions 62, 67
                    (No. 78-009, Nov. 7, 1978)

     As Mr. Siemens owns fewer than three rental units, the
ordinance under consideration by the Los Gatos City Council
will affect his interests in substantially the same manner
as it will affect a significant segment of the public. He
therefore does not have to disqualify himself from consideration
of this ordinance.

     I have enclosed a copy of the Ferraro opinion. If you
have any further questions, please do not hesitate to call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Thomas M. Sobel
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

TMS:nrw
Enclosure

sections.question: Whether Mr. Siemens can participate in the City Council's consideration of the ordinance given that he owns residential real property including a small dwelling which he rents out for $220 per month, in light of the City Council's proposal to enact a rent control ordinance.
sections.conclusion: As Mr. Siemens owns fewer than three rental units, the ordinance under consideration by the Los Gatos City Council will affect his interests in substantially the same manner as it will affect a significant segment of the public. He therefore does not have to disqualify himself from consideration of this ordinance.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a city council member who owns a small rental property (one unit renting for $220/month) participate in voting on a rent control ordinance, or must he disqualify himself due to financial interest?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The council member does not have a disqualifying financial interest and may participate in the rent control ordinance vote, as owners of three or fewer rental units constitute a significant segment of the public and are affected similarly to the general public.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a formal advice letter confirming prior telephone advice. The question and conclusion are clearly present but embedded in letter format rather than in formal sections. The legal analysis extensively references Government Code Section 87100, 87103 and prior FPPC Opinion No. 78-009 (Ferraro).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Mr. Siemens can participate in the City Council's consideration of the ordinance given that he owns residential real property including a small dwelling which he rents out for $220 per month, in light of the City Council's proposal to enact a rent control ordinance.

CONCLUSION: As Mr. Siemens owns fewer than three rental units, the ordinance under consideration by the Los Gatos City Council will affect his interests in substantially the same manner as it will affect a significant segment of the public. He therefore does not have to disqualify himself from consideration of this ordinance.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that a Los Gatos City Council member who owns one small rental property ($220/month) need not disqualify himself from voting on a proposed rent control ordinance, because small landlords (three or fewer units) are a significant segment of the public affected similarly to others.
